---
title: "09 · Convex Optimisation"
subtitle: "When the loss landscape has a single bottom — and how we know"
lesson-id: "t6.convex-optimization"
tier: 6
prerequisites: ["t5.partial-derivatives", "t5.eigenvalues"]
unlocks: ["t6.gradient-methods"]
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 30
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [T5 L05 — Partial Derivatives](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/05-partial-derivatives/index.qmd)
   and [T5 L04 — Eigenvalues](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/04-eigenvalues/index.qmd)?
2. Comfortable with gradients and the Hessian matrix (T4 L06 S1)?
3. Recall positive-definite matrices (eigenvalues > 0)?
:::

## Touch

Pick a function. Draw a straight line between any two points on its
graph. The widget tells you whether the curve always sits *below* that
line — bowl-shaped — or whether it pokes through somewhere.

```{ojs}
viewof cvSel = Inputs.select(["f(x) = x²: convex (parabola)", "f(x) = e^x: strictly convex", "f(x) = |x|: convex but not differentiable", "f(x) = sin(x): NOT convex", "f(x) = x³: NOT convex on ℝ"], {value: "f(x) = x²: convex (parabola)", label: "Function:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  let info;
  switch(cvSel) {
    case "f(x) = x²: convex (parabola)": info = "Twice differentiable; f''(x) = 2 ≥ 0 everywhere. **Strictly convex.** Unique global min at x = 0."; break;
    case "f(x) = e^x: strictly convex": info = "f''(x) = e^x > 0. Strictly convex. No min on R (decreases as x → −∞)."; break;
    case "f(x) = |x|: convex but not differentiable": info = "Convex (kink at 0 still satisfies chord inequality), but f' undefined at 0. Subgradient methods handle this."; break;
    case "f(x) = sin(x): NOT convex": info = "Oscillates between concave and convex regions. No global structure."; break;
    case "f(x) = x³: NOT convex on ℝ": info = "f''(x) = 6x: positive for x > 0, negative for x < 0. Convex on [0, ∞) only."; break;
  }
  return html`<div style="margin: 1rem 0;">
    <div style="font-family:monospace; font-size:0.95rem; color:#c2185b; text-align:center;">${info}</div>
  </div>`;
}
```

## Wonder

1. Why is "convex" so important for optimisation?
2. Quadratic functions $f(x) = (1/2) x^T A x + b^T x + c$ — when are
   they convex?
3. Are sums of convex functions convex?

## Discover

### Problem 1

A function $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is convex iff $f''(x) \ge 0$
everywhere (for twice-differentiable $f$).

Verify $f(x) = x^4$ is convex.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$f''(x) = 12 x^2 \ge 0$ ✓. Convex.

(*Strictly* convex except at $x = 0$.)
</details>

### Problem 2

For multivariate $f$, **convex iff Hessian $\nabla^2 f$ is positive
semi-definite** (eigenvalues $\ge 0$).

Determine: is $f(x, y) = x^2 + 2 x y + 3 y^2$ convex?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$H = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 6 \end{pmatrix}$. Eigenvalues?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\det(H - \lambda I) = (2 - \lambda)(6 - \lambda) - 4 = \lambda^2 - 8
\lambda + 8 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = 4 \pm 2 \sqrt{2} \approx 6.83,
1.17$. Both positive ⇒ Hessian PD ⇒ **strictly convex**.
</details>

### Problem 3

**Why convexity matters:** every local minimum of a convex function
is a *global* minimum.

Sketch: if $x^*$ is a local min and $y$ has $f(y) < f(x^*)$, then on
the segment from $x^*$ to $y$, by convexity, $f$ stays $\le f(y) <
f(x^*)$ near $x^*$ — contradicting local min.

Why is this practically huge?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**No "saddle" or "local minimum" traps.** Gradient descent (and many
other algorithms) on convex functions reach the global optimum
*reliably*. For non-convex problems (most real ML), you might get
stuck.

This is why much of machine learning theory focuses on convex
problems first: results there are clean, then extended (with
caveats) to non-convex.
</details>

### Problem 4

**Operations preserving convexity:**

- Sum of convex functions is convex.
- $\max\{f_1, f_2\}$ of convex functions is convex.
- Composition $g \circ f$: convex if $g$ is convex *non-decreasing*
  and $f$ is convex (e.g.).

Show: $f(x) = \max(0, x)$ (ReLU) is convex.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
ReLU is the max of two convex functions: $0$ (constant — convex) and
$x$ (linear — convex). Max of convex is convex.

ReLU is convex but not strictly convex (flat for $x < 0$). Still,
it's the standard activation in deep networks.
</details>

### Problem 5

**Convex optimisation problems** have well-developed theory:

- **Linear Programming (LP):** linear objective, linear constraints.
  Polynomial-time algorithms.
- **Quadratic Programming (QP):** quadratic objective, linear
  constraints. Solved efficiently.
- **Convex programming (general):** convex objective, convex
  constraints. Polynomial-time interior-point methods.

Why is "non-convex" a hard line in computer science?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Non-convex problems may have many local minima, requiring
exponential time in the worst case.** Many NP-hard problems are
non-convex.

But many *practically* important problems (LP, semidefinite, certain
ML losses for shallow models) are convex. Modern ML is mostly *non-
convex* — yet works well in practice via stochastic gradient methods
and architectural tricks.

Understanding **why non-convex deep learning works** is one of the
biggest open theoretical questions in AI.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
**Linear programming was born in wartime.** Soviet mathematician
**Leonid Kantorovich** invented LP in 1939 while consulting for the
Leningrad plywood trust on how to allocate machines and raw timber
optimally — convex optimisation, decades before the West had a name
for it. His pamphlet circulated quietly through Soviet planning
offices for years. In 1947 the American **George Dantzig**, working
on US Air Force logistics, independently invented the **simplex
method** for the same problem. Then in 1984, **Narendra Karmarkar**
(at Bell Labs) shocked the field with the first **polynomial-time
interior-point algorithm**, beating simplex's exponential worst case
and triggering the modern convex-optimisation renaissance.
Kantorovich shared the 1975 Nobel Prize in Economics for his work.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
A convex landscape is one where every "downhill" step is a step
toward the *global* bottom. There are no false valleys, no traps,
no surprises. This is the entire reason convex optimisation is
clean: walking downhill always works, because there is only one
"down."
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

A set $C \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ is **convex** if for $x, y \in C$
and $t \in [0, 1]$: $(1 - t) x + t y \in C$.

A function $f$ is **convex** on $C$ if for $x, y \in C, t \in [0, 1]$:
$$f((1-t) x + t y) \le (1-t) f(x) + t f(y).$$

For twice-differentiable $f$:

- 1D: convex iff $f'' \ge 0$.
- $\mathbb{R}^n$: convex iff Hessian $\nabla^2 f$ is positive
  semi-definite.

**Strict convexity:** strict inequality for $t \in (0, 1), x \neq y$.

**Properties:**

- **Local min = global min** for convex $f$.
- Sum, max, non-negative-scaling of convex are convex.
- Composition with monotone functions sometimes preserves convexity.

**Convex optimisation problem:**
$$\min_{x \in C} f(x), \quad C \text{ convex}, f \text{ convex}.$$

**Common classes:** linear (LP), quadratic (QP), semidefinite (SDP),
general convex (interior-point methods).

**Duality:** primal problem ↔ dual problem; under regularity, equal
optima.

:::

> *Convex = lies below chords; convex set = closed under midpoints;*
> *Convex optimisation: local min = global min; tractable;*
> *Non-convex: hard, but ML works in practice via SGD.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Show $f(x) = e^x + x^2$ is convex.

$f''(x) = e^x + 2 > 0$ for all $x$. **Strictly convex.**

(Sum of convex functions $e^x$ and $x^2$ is convex.)

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Is $f(x) = -x^2$ convex?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$f''(x) = -2 < 0$. **No** (concave).
</details>

**W2.** Convex set: $\{x : x \ge 0\}$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Yes** (half-line).
</details>

**W3.** Convex set: union of two intervals?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Generally **no** (midpoint between intervals isn't included).
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Is the unit ball $\{x : \|x\| \le 1\}$ convex?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Yes. By triangle inequality: $\|t x + (1-t) y\| \le t \|x\| + (1-t)
\|y\| \le 1$.
</details>

**P2.** Convex Hessian for $f(x, y) = 5 x^2 + 4 x y + 3 y^2$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$H = \begin{pmatrix} 10 & 4 \\ 4 & 6 \end{pmatrix}$. Det = 60 - 16 = 44
> 0; trace 16 > 0 ⇒ both eigenvalues positive ⇒ **convex**.
</details>

**P3.** Is the function $f(x) = -\log x$ (for $x > 0$) convex?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$f''(x) = 1/x^2 > 0$. **Convex.** (Used in interior-point methods as
a "barrier function" enforcing $x > 0$.)
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** **Why is least squares regression convex?** $L(\beta) = \|y -
X\beta\|^2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Quadratic in $\beta$: $\beta^T X^T X \beta - 2 y^T X \beta + y^T y$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$L(\beta) = \beta^T (X^T X) \beta - 2 (X^T y)^T \beta + y^T y$.

Hessian: $2 X^T X$, which is **positive semi-definite** (Gram
matrix). So $L$ is convex.

Hence linear regression has a *unique* (or affine subspace of)
minima — solvable in closed form (normal equations T5 L02 S2).
This is why classical regression is so well-behaved.
</details>

**S2.** **In modern ML, neural network losses are NOT convex.** Why
does training still work?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Several theoretical reasons** (active research):

1. *Over-parameterisation:* enough capacity → loss landscape becomes
   "benign" (saddle points easy to escape, minima are wide and
   equivalent).
2. *Stochastic gradient noise:* SGD's noise helps escape saddles.
3. *Implicit bias:* gradient descent finds particular nice minima
   (e.g., low-norm in linear models).
4. *Architecture:* skip connections (ResNets) and normalisation
   (BatchNorm) shape the loss landscape favourably.

Despite non-convexity, modern deep nets reach (essentially) global
minima reliably. **Why** is one of the biggest open theoretical
questions in modern ML.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Convex set and convex function are the same."** Different
   things. A function is convex; its **epigraph** (region above its
   graph) is a convex set.
2. **"Convex = bowl-shaped only."** Higher dimensions: convex shapes
   include simplices, polyhedra, balls, ellipsoids — many forms.
3. **"All ML is non-convex."** Linear regression, logistic
   regression, SVM, many generative models *are* convex. Deep nets
   are not.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **A [convex function](/glossary.qmd#convex-function) lies below
   every chord** — bowl-shaped, no internal bumps; every local minimum
   is automatically the global minimum.
2. **The Hessian PSD test** (eigenvalues $\ge 0$, via the
   [eigenvalues](/glossary.qmd#eigenvalue) of $\nabla^2 f$) is the
   workhorse check for convexity in higher dimensions.
3. **Convex problems are tractable; non-convex problems are not** —
   yet modern deep learning works empirically because over-
   parameterisation, SGD noise, and architecture flatten the
   landscape.

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you state when a 1D function is convex?
2. Without scrolling back, is ReLU convex?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

(Answer: yes.)

- **3 yes:** ready for **[10 · Gradient Methods](../10-gradient-methods/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **S1** (least squares).
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P3 and P5*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[T5 L05 — Partial Derivatives](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/05-partial-derivatives/index.qmd)**
- **[T5 L04 — Eigenvalues](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/04-eigenvalues/index.qmd)** —
  for Hessian PD test.

### What this unlocks

- **[10 · Gradient Methods](../10-gradient-methods/index.qmd)**

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / Machine Learning.** Classical convex methods (linear/logistic
regression, SVM); modern (non-convex) deep learning that works
"empirically convex" via tricks (S2).
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Operations research.** LP, QP, network flow, scheduling,
portfolio optimisation — all rest on convex optimisation theory.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Mathematical research.** Convex analysis is a polished subject
(Rockafellar's *Convex Analysis*). Deep ties to functional analysis
and game theory.
:::
